articles.label: Public Records
articles.headline: Escambia County's $2.1M legal bill: What the public records fights are really about
articles.dek: The county has spent more defending against records requests than releasing them. A Flightline analysis of invoices and court filings shows who's asking, who's billing, and what officials don't want you to see.
brief: Escambia County has spent $2.1 million on legal fees related to public records disputes since January 2023, according to invoices obtained by The Flightline
Three law firms have billed the county for work fighting records requests, with one firm—Allen Norton & Blue—accounting for $1.4 million of the total
The most expensive disputes involve communications between commissioners and developers, personnel records from the county administrator's office, and sheriff's department internal affairs files
articles.body:
The invoice arrived at the Escambia County Clerk's office on March 3. Sixteen pages. $47,832.50 for February alone. The billing attorney had logged 127 hours that month on a single case—a lawsuit filed by a Pensacola resident who wanted emails between Commissioner Jeff Bergosh and a Perdido Key developer.

The resident's original records request had been four sentences long.

Since January 2023, Escambia County has spent $2.1 million on legal fees defending against public records lawsuits and appeals, according to a Flightline analysis of 847 invoices obtained through—perhaps fittingly—a public records request. The county's own records staff, by contrast, operates on an annual budget of $340,000.

The math is simple. Escambia County has spent more money fighting requests than fulfilling them.

Who's billing

Three law firms have collected the bulk of the fees. Allen Norton & Blue, a Pensacola firm that handles labor and employment matters for the county, billed $1.4 million across 23 separate records-related cases. Sniffen & Spellman, based in Tallahassee, invoiced $412,000 for appellate work. Clark Partington, another Pensacola firm, collected $298,000 for cases involving the sheriff's office.

The Flightline requested billing records from all three firms. Allen Norton & Blue declined to comment, citing attorney-client privilege. Sniffen & Spellman did not respond to multiple requests. Clark Partington partner Michael Kozlowski said the firm's invoices "reflect the complexity of the legal issues involved" but would not elaborate.

County Attorney Alison Rogers defended the expenditures at a commission meeting last month. "These aren't simple requests," she said. "We're dealing with sophisticated requesters who are often represented by counsel. We have an obligation to protect exemptions established by state law."

The invoices tell a different story. Line items show attorneys billing $350 per hour to review whether emails about road paving schedules could be withheld. One entry: "Research re: applicability of security system exemption to discussion of pothole locations. 2.3 hours."

Who's asking

The county does not publicly track who files records requests. The Flightline compiled a list by cross-referencing court filings with county correspondence logs.

The most frequent litigants are not journalists or activists. They're people with specific grievances against the county—former employees, property owners in disputes over zoning decisions, contractors who lost bids.

Michael Trent, a Milton resident, has filed 14 lawsuits against the county since 2021, all stemming from a 2020 decision to deny a permit for his RV storage business. His requests have focused on communications between county staff and the property owners who opposed his application.

"They've spent more fighting me than my property is worth," Trent said in an interview. "That tells you something."

Trent has won three of his cases. The county has won five. Six remain pending.

The most expensive single case involves a nonprofit called Gulf Coast Accountability Project, which requested all communications between commissioners and representatives of a development group that purchased 1,200 acres near Beulah in 2024. The county initially estimated it would take 18 months to fulfill the request. Gulf Coast Accountability sued. The case has been in litigation for 14 months. Legal fees so far: $312,000.

The development group, Beulah Land Partners LLC, has since received preliminary approval for a 3,400-unit residential community.

What they're protecting

Florida's public records law is among the broadest in the country. The state constitution establishes a presumption of openness. Exemptions exist but must be narrowly construed.

The county has invoked exemptions in roughly 60 percent of the disputed cases, according to court filings. The most common: attorney-client privilege, the security systems exemption, and personnel records protections.

Judges have rejected the county's exemption claims in 22 of 31 cases that reached a ruling. In seven cases, judges issued sanctions against the county for what they deemed frivolous withholding.

Circuit Judge Gary Bergosh—no relation to the commissioner—wrote in a September 2025 order that the county had "adopted a reflexive posture of resistance" to records requests. The case involved emails about a contract for jail food services. The county had claimed the emails were exempt because they contained "proprietary business information." The emails discussed meatloaf.

"The claimed exemption is not close to applicable," Bergosh wrote. "The court is left to conclude that the county's position is not based on a good-faith interpretation of the law."

He ordered the county to pay $18,000 in the requester's attorney fees.

The commissioner question

Five of the ten most expensive cases involve communications to or from Commissioner Bergosh, who has served on the board since 2012. Bergosh did not respond to interview requests for this story.

At a March commission meeting, Bergosh addressed the records disputes during public comment period—an unusual move for a sitting commissioner.

"I'm being targeted," he said. "These are fishing expeditions by people who don't like the decisions this board has made. We have a right to defend ourselves."

Commissioner Lumon May, who has been named in two records suits, offered a different view. "The public has a right to know how we make decisions," May said in an interview. "If we're spending millions of dollars to stop them from finding out, maybe we should ask why."

The commission has not held a public discussion about the legal costs. The expenditures do not appear as a separate line item in the county budget. They are absorbed into the general legal services allocation, which has increased 34 percent since 2022.

What happens next

Three bills currently moving through the Florida Legislature would affect how counties handle records disputes. One would cap attorney fee awards in records cases. Another would extend the time agencies have to respond to requests from 10 days to 30. A third would create new exemptions for communications between elected officials and their personal attorneys.

The Florida Press Association has opposed all three. The Florida Association of Counties has endorsed them.

In Escambia County, the bills are being watched closely. County Administrator Wes Moreno said in a budget presentation last month that legal costs "continue to be a challenge." He proposed hiring a full-time attorney dedicated to records matters. Estimated salary: $125,000.

The commission tabled the proposal pending further review.

Meanwhile, the invoices keep arriving. April's bills are due next week. Allen Norton & Blue has already submitted a preliminary estimate: $52,000.

The Flightline has requested an itemized breakdown. The county has 10 days to respond.

articles.label: Development
articles.headline: The hole on Palafox turns three
articles.dek: The demolished Brent Building site has sat empty since 2023. The developer says financing fell through. The city says permits are pending. Meanwhile, downtown's busiest block has a chain-link fence for a neighbor.
brief: The former Brent Building site at 1 South Palafox has remained a fenced-off dirt lot since demolition completed in April 2023
Developer Maritime Holdings cites commercial lending conditions; city records show permit applications expired in 2024 and were never renewed
Downtown business owners report customer complaints and question whether the project will ever break ground
articles.body:
A rat emerged from beneath the plywood barrier at 1 South Palafox Place on a Tuesday afternoon, scurried past a faded rendering of a six-story mixed-use tower, and disappeared into a gap in the chain-link fence. A woman walking toward the Saenger Theatre stepped around it without breaking stride. This is normal now.

Three years ago this month, crews finished demolishing the Brent Building, a 1960s-era office structure that had housed insurance agencies and law firms for decades. The developer, Maritime Holdings LLC, promised a $42 million project: ground-floor retail, four floors of Class A office space, a rooftop restaurant with bay views. Construction was supposed to begin by fall 2023.

The hole remains.

What the records show

Escambia County property records list Maritime Holdings as the current owner, with a taxable value of $1.2 million for the vacant parcel. The company, registered in Delaware with a mailing address in Atlanta, purchased the site in 2021 for $3.8 million.

City of Pensacola permit records tell a different story than the one Maritime has offered publicly. The company filed for site development permits in August 2023. Those permits expired in February 2024 after no construction activity occurred. No renewal applications appear in the city's online portal.

Kayla Whitfield, a spokesperson for the city's Development Services Department, confirmed the lapsed permits in an email. She said the city has had "limited recent communication" with the developer but could not provide specifics, citing ongoing discussions.

"We remain hopeful that this site will be developed in a manner consistent with downtown's growth," Whitfield wrote. "However, the city cannot compel a private property owner to build on their land."

The developer's explanation

Maritime Holdings did not respond to three phone calls and two emails seeking comment over a two-week period. The company's website still displays the original 2022 renderings of the proposed tower, along with a "Coming Soon" banner.

In a December 2024 interview with the Pensacola News Journal, Maritime principal David Ashworth blamed the delay on commercial lending conditions. Interest rates had climbed. Regional banks had pulled back from speculative office development. The project, he said then, was "paused, not canceled."

That was sixteen months ago. Ashworth has not spoken publicly about the project since.

The view from next door

Rebecca Tran has managed Gallery Night operations for downtown Pensacola since 2019. Her office window on the second floor of the Blount Building looks directly at the vacant lot.

"I watch people take photos of it," she said. "Not because it's interesting. Because it's bizarre. You have this thriving block—the Saenger, the restaurants, all these people walking around—and then this hole with weeds growing in it."

Tran said she's fielded questions from visiting artists and vendors who assume the lot is a stalled city project. She's stopped trying to explain.

At The Tuscan Oven, half a block south on Palafox, owner Michael Grieco said customers occasionally ask about the fence. "They want to know if it's going to be a parking garage," he said. "I tell them I have no idea. Nobody does."

Grieco has operated his restaurant since 2016. He remembers the Brent Building as unremarkable but functional—a place where office workers grabbed lunch. He was optimistic when demolition began.

"We all were," he said. "More foot traffic, more residents downtown. That was the pitch."

Now he's skeptical anything will happen before he retires. He's 58.

A pattern downtown

The Palafox lot is not the only stalled project in Pensacola's urban core, though it is the most visible. The former Pensacola News Journal building on Romana Street has sat vacant since 2018, its future unclear despite multiple announced redevelopment plans. A proposed hotel on Jefferson Street broke ground in 2022 and stopped work seven months later; that site remains partially excavated.

City Councilwoman Jennifer Brahier, whose District 6 includes downtown, acknowledged frustration among residents and business owners. In an interview, she said the council has limited tools to address private development delays.

"We can't force someone to build," she said. "What we can do is make sure the regulatory process is ready when they are. And we have done that."

Brahier said she has personally reached out to Maritime Holdings twice in the past year and received no response.

The city does enforce nuisance ordinances on vacant properties. Records show code enforcement officers have cited the Palafox lot twice since 2023—once for overgrown vegetation, once for an unsecured barrier panel. Maritime paid both fines, totaling $475.

What happens next

Nothing, probably. At least not soon.

Commercial real estate brokers who spoke on background said the Pensacola office market cannot currently support speculative construction. Vacancy rates downtown hover near 18 percent, according to CoStar data. Remote work patterns have not reversed. No major employer has announced relocation plans.

"You'd need a tenant signed before any bank would touch this," one broker said. "And even then, the numbers are tight."

Maritime Holdings has not listed the property for sale. No other developers have filed permits for the site.

The chain-link fence, installed as temporary construction security in April 2023, has become permanent. The renderings bolted to the plywood are sun-bleached now, the rooftop diners in the illustration faded to ghosts. The rat, presumably, is still there.

articles.label: Military
articles.headline: Blue Angels homecoming week arrives with new flight path complaints from Gulf Breeze
articles.dek: Practice runs for the July air show have already started. So have the calls to city hall. After last year's noise study went nowhere, residents are trying a different approach.
brief: Blue Angels spring practice season began April 14, with homecoming show scheduled for July 10-11
Gulf Breeze residents report flight paths shifted south this year, bringing jets closer to Tiger Point and Midway subdivisions
A citizen petition requesting formal flight path review has gathered over 800 signatures in one week
articles.body:
The roar hit Tiger Point at 11:47 a.m. Tuesday. Six F/A-18 Super Hornets in delta formation passed low enough over Shoreline Drive that Margaret Weiss felt the vibration in her kitchen cabinets before she heard the engines.

"My dog hid under the bed for three hours," Weiss said. "Last year they came over maybe once a week during practice. This is every day now. Multiple times."

The Blue Angels returned to Naval Air Station Pensacola on April 14 for their annual spring training cycle ahead of the July 10-11 homecoming air show. Practice runs began the same day. So did the phone calls to Gulf Breeze City Hall.

City Manager Samantha Holbrook said her office logged 47 noise complaints between April 14 and April 18. That's more than the city received during the entire 2025 practice season.

"The volume of calls is notable," Holbrook said. "What's also notable is that many of these callers are not people we've heard from before. These aren't the usual voices."

A question of altitude

Residents in the Tiger Point, Midway, and South Santa Rosa subdivisions say something changed. The jets aren't just loud. They're closer.

Don Patterson has lived on Peninsula Drive for 22 years. He served as an aviation electrician's mate in the Navy and spent two decades working as a ground crew supervisor at Pensacola Regional Airport. He knows what aircraft sound like at different altitudes.

"These are not 2,500-foot passes," Patterson said. "I'd put them at 1,200, maybe 1,500 feet when they come over Tiger Point. That's practice altitude, not transit altitude."

The Blue Angels public affairs office did not respond to three requests for comment on current flight paths and training altitudes. A NAS Pensacola spokesperson provided a written statement that did not address the specific questions.

"The Blue Angels are proud to call Pensacola home and appreciate the community's support," the statement read. "Flight operations are conducted in accordance with established safety protocols and airspace agreements."

Those airspace agreements—negotiated decades ago between the Navy, the FAA, and local governments—are not public documents. Multiple public records requests filed by residents since 2023 have been denied, with the Navy citing operational security.

Last year's study

This isn't the first time Gulf Breeze residents have raised concerns about Blue Angels flight patterns. In September 2024, the city council voted 4-1 to commission an independent noise study after a summer of complaints. The council allocated $35,000 from reserve funds.

The study never happened.

According to city records obtained by The Flightline, the only firm that submitted a qualified bid—Acoustic Environmental Consultants of Tampa—withdrew in January 2025 after the Navy declined to provide flight path data or allow sound monitoring equipment within three miles of NAS property.

"Without baseline flight data, there's no way to produce a meaningful study," AEC principal Robert Tran wrote in his withdrawal letter. "We would essentially be measuring noise without any ability to correlate it to specific operations."

The $35,000 was returned to reserves. The council did not publicly discuss the study's failure.

Mayor Pro Tem David Lindquist, who cast the lone vote against the study in 2024, said he wasn't surprised.

"The Navy isn't obligated to cooperate with us on this," Lindquist said. "I said at the time it was a waste of money. The federal government doesn't answer to Gulf Breeze."

A different approach

Sarah Chen moved to Tiger Point in 2021 from San Diego. Her husband is a civilian engineer at NAS Pensacola. She describes herself as "pro-Navy" and says she knew what she was signing up for when they bought a house in northwest Florida.

"I'm not trying to shut down the Blue Angels," Chen said. "I'm asking whether the current flight paths are the only option."

On April 15, Chen created an online petition requesting that the Gulf Breeze City Council formally ask the Navy for a flight path review. Not a noise study. Not a confrontation. A review.

The petition gathered 847 signatures in its first week. Chen plans to present it at the May 4 council meeting.

"The framing matters," Chen said. "Last time, it became adversarial. Residents versus the Navy. That's not going to work. We need to approach this as neighbors asking for a conversation."

The petition specifically requests three things: that the Navy provide a general explanation of how practice flight paths are determined, that the city formally ask whether residential growth in south Santa Rosa County has been factored into current routing, and that residents be given a point of contact for ongoing concerns.

"It's not unreasonable," Chen said. "We're not asking them to stop flying. We're asking them to talk to us."

The economic reality

Any discussion of Blue Angels operations in Pensacola comes with an economic asterisk. The homecoming air show is the largest annual tourism event in Escambia County. The 2024 show drew an estimated 180,000 spectators over two days and generated an estimated $27 million in direct spending, according to Visit Pensacola.

NAS Pensacola employs approximately 16,000 military and civilian personnel. The base contributes an estimated $3.2 billion annually to the regional economy.

Gulf Breeze City Council member Patricia Rowan, whose district includes Tiger Point, said she's sympathetic to residents but cautious about the political dynamics.

"We have to be strategic," Rowan said. "If Gulf Breeze becomes known as the city that complains about the Blue Angels, that has consequences. Whether that's fair or not, it's reality."

Rowan said she would support adding Chen's petition to the May 4 agenda but stopped short of committing to a formal city request to the Navy.

"Let's hear from residents first," she said. "Let's see what the council thinks."

What comes next

The Blue Angels will practice over Pensacola through June, typically two to three times per week. The tempo increases in late June as the homecoming show approaches. Practice sessions are weather-dependent and not publicly scheduled in advance.

Weiss, the Tiger Point resident whose dog spent Tuesday afternoon under the bed, said she's signed Chen's petition but doesn't expect much.

"I've lived here since 1998," Weiss said. "The Navy does what the Navy does. But maybe if enough people say something, they'll at least know we're here."

Chen has a different view.

"I grew up in San Diego," she said. "Naval Air Station North Island, Top Gun, all of it. Those communities found ways to work with the Navy. It's not impossible. It just requires everyone to act like adults."

The Gulf Breeze City Council meets May 4 at 6 p.m. at City Hall on Shoreline Drive. Public comment is scheduled for the first 30 minutes.
